mission.title: Install and Configure Paralus on Kubernetes
mission.description: Paralus is an all-in-one Kubernetes access manager that provides user-level credentials, RBAC, SSO, and audit logs. Installing Paralus allows you to manage secure access to your Kubernetes infrastructure effectively.

steps.title: Add the Paralus Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Paralus Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add paralus https://paralus.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Paralus using Helm
steps.description:
Install Paralus in the `paralus` namespace (it will be created if it does not exist) with the following command:
```bash
helm install paralus paralus/paralus --namespace paralus --create-namespace --version v0.3.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Paralus pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace paralus
```

steps.title: Port forward to access Paralus GUI
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Paralus GUI from your local machine:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/paralus --namespace paralus 11000:11000
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Paralus will show the Paralus pods running in the `paralus` namespace, and you will be able to access the Paralus GUI via port forwarding on localhost:11000.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add paralus https://paralus.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
helm install paralus paralus/paralus --namespace paralus --create-namespace --version v0.3.0

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Paralus release from the Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources created by the Helm chart.
```bash
helm uninstall paralus --namespace paralus
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. This ensures that all resources related to Paralus are removed.
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep paralus | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace paralus
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Paralus namespace and verify that it has been removed from the cluster. This step is crucial for cleaning up the environment.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace paralus
kubectl get namespaces | grep paralus
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Paralus deployment. You can use the following command to export the current configuration.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace paralus -o yaml > paralus-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Paralus installation to the latest version. Make sure to specify the desired version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade paralus paralus/paralus --namespace paralus --version v0.4.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Paralus pods are running the new version successfully after the upgrade. This ensures that the upgrade was successful.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace paralus
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Paralus pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors that may indicate the cause. Use the following command to view the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace paralus
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Paralus GUI, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace paralus
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If the pods are not starting due to resource constraints, check the resource limits and requests set in the deployment. You can view the current configuration with:
```bash
kubectl get deployment paralus --namespace paralus -o yaml
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, ensure that you are using the correct namespace. List all releases in the namespace with:
```bash
helm list --namespace paralus
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
